oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Screening, cleaning and quarantining: Biosecurity in small ruminant practice
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Pesato, Michael
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: small ruminants
biosecurity
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Biosecurity is an essential concern for all food animal practi­tioners. In small ruminant practice, three primary diseases are important for biosecurity of the entire flock or herd. Testing for these primary diseases allows clients and practitioners to screen for infectious diseases. These diseases include caseous lymphad­enitis, Johne’s disease, and small ruminant lentiviruses. Clean­ing is another essential element of biosecurity. An ideal environ­ment allows for cleaning and disinfection (concrete flooring and walls, rubber mats, metal gates) but most small ruminant enclo­sures are difficult to clean and disinfect due wooden structures with dirt flooring. Cleaning should always come before disinfec­tion, and tuberculocidal cleaning products are ideal. Quarantine is the final element of a biosecurity program. Ideally, a quaran­tine period of 1-month allows for new animal acclimation as well as veterinary inspection and diagnostic testing.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 201-203
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
